I think it does implicitly in the following check (util.py:770):
```python
    if intervals.ndim != 2 or intervals.shape[1] != 2:
        raise ValueError('Intervals should be n-by-2 numpy ndarray, '
                         'but shape={}'.format(intervals.shape))
```
Here, `np.atleast_2d(np.array([])).shape[1] == 0`, and thus the `ValueError` will be raised. So, at least one interval must be present, and it must have a positive duration, and thus `reference_intervals[-1, 1] - reference_intervals[0, 0]` must be positive.

This is a bit tricky. In finance you have something which is called "discounted cash flow", which is a way to show that money tomorrow doesn't equal money today. Basically you discount (i.e. divide) future earnings at a discount rate (i.e. (1+d)^n, where d = monthly discount rate, n = month in which you earn that money). The most used interest rate is the monthly one (hence I divide the days used to figure out the number of future expected purchases by 30 -> average number of days in a month). In case we wanted to change it to the annual rate then the relationship is: monthly rate = (1 + annual rate)^(1/12) - 1. Let me know your take on this

@eric-haibin-lin For now this is added for testing only, but it's actually quite useful, and not just for the TensorRT integration, but potentially for other acceleration libraries in the future. Imagine wanting to visualize a model to figure out what the acceleration library did. Looking at the timeline may be helpful, but only if one knows which kernels belong to the accelerator, and which to MXNet or direct cuBLAS/cuDNN calls. Seeing how the graph got rewritten can help determine which layers could not be handled by the acceleration library, to determine what asks to make of libraries going forward. For example, TensorRT doesn't handle non-maximum suppression (NMS) directly, it can do that via a plugin. This initial integration doesn't handle TensorRT plugins. Knowing what didn't get subsumed into the accelerator graph node and comparing against a profile can determine whether for instance layers that are not being handled by the acceleration library subgraph are dominating the profile - a visual inspection makes this really quick. This could be useful for both the Graphviz integration, as well as potentially for TensorBoard. We tested this with GraphViz already, and it's working fine. Check out the attached visualization of LeNet-5. The graph on the left is the original MXNet one, and the one on the right is with all layers except for the input tensor having been replaced by a single TensorRT node. For a more complex network such as SSD, it would be visible that the NMS layer is a separate NNVM node, outside of the TensorRT subgraph.

I don't think so.  The problem is that this test is checking the routines that render the surface brightness of the profile without convolving by the pixel.  That's where there is a sharp cliff in the profile that can be very close to a pixel center, in which case the FFT method can sometimes end up getting slightly different results at those edge pixels due to numerical rounding.

The normal way you would want to render a `TopHat` is:
```
tophat = galsim.TopHat(radius)
tophat.drawImage(image = image, offset = offset, ...)
```
which implicitly convolves by the `Pixel` profile behind the scenes to correctly render what you would see when shining through a pinhole onto a CCD.  

So I don't think there are any special caveats in this case.  We already spend a while talking about how convolution by a pixel is required to get an accurate rendering and how that's what drawImage does by default (with `method='auto'`).

```py
def Quantile(q, variable, weight_variable = None, filter_variable = None):
    def formula(entity, period):
        value = entity(variable, period)
        if weight_variable is not None:
            weight = entity(weight_variable, period)
        weight = entity.filled_array(1)
        if filter_variable is not None:
            filter_value = entity(filter_variable, period)
            weight = filter_value * weight

        labels = arange(1, q + 1)
        quantile, _ = mark_weighted_percentiles(
            value,
            labels,
            weight,
            method = 2,  # * filter,
            return_quantiles = True,
            )
        if filter_variable is not None:
            quantile = where(weight > 0, quantile, -1)
        return quantile

    return formula

class decile_salaire_from_quantile(Variable):
    value_type = int
    entity = Individu
    label = u"Décile de salaire nouveau calcul"
    definition_period = YEAR
    formula = Quantile(10, 'salaire')
```

Hi @skliarpawlo. Thanks for the feedback - is there anything specific you'd change from the function signature to make it easier to understand / use? Also consider that this PR just adds one optional argument (`operand`) in a backwards compatible improvement, so introducing a completely new dsl would be kinda out of scope here.

Having said that, Scott provides a good usecase above. For example, think of a case where you have a continuous age column and you want to transform it into discrete, pre-defined buckets. Then your configuration might specify something like
```
AGE_BUCKETS = [(13, 17), (18, 24), (25, 34), ...]
```
In your code, this allows you to say
```
switch_case(
    'age', 
    case={bucket_limits: '_'.join(bucket_limits) for bucket_limits in AGE_BUCKETS}, 
    operand=lambda c, v: c.between(*v),
)
```
which is very compact (and straightforward once you get familiar with what the arguments are). It is way more difficult to chain `when` statements together that way, especially when you can't list explicitly all the cases. 

Even when you can list explicitly all cases though, I've found `switch_case` to be much more convenient for readability purposes in the usual case, as it omits a lot of the boilerplate and lists the cases directly for the reader to skim over. But that's just a personal preference.

The reason I don't pass cost into functions, e.g. compute_BtBinv, is because the cost is all tracked in WUs on the current grid, which means it is respective to the number of nonzeros in A. Many of these functions do not take in A as a parameter, which means I either need to also pass in A.nnz, pass in a temp variable to get FLOPs, then let cost += temp/A.nnz, or track global complexity only in FLOPs. I didn't like passing in A.nnz because it's extra parameters; didn't like the counting FLOPs via cost[] in a function, as it would be inconsistent with the rest of the functions that count WUs in cost[]; and didn't like the complexity counting all in FLOPs instead of WUs partially due to overflow concern, and because something about the whole thing made more sense in WUs to me.

So, I'm happy to discuss modifications, but, it's not quite as simple as just adding cost[] to the extra functions.

Good eye! You're seeing the discrepancy with the third byte I believe, \x20 (in the spec) vs \x00 (here in the test). This is actually ok.

For these example encodings, the spec actually gives everything the dummy tag (the first 3 bytes of the encoding) \x42\x00\x20 (see the leading text in Section 9.1.2 of the spec). This tag is actually a legitimate tag for a CompromiseDate object (see Table 213, specifically pg. 107 of the spec). However, I didn't want all of my default primitives to have the tag for a legitimate KMIP object; I want a neutral tag that can apply to anything. So instead, I made up a new default tag, \x42\x00\x00, which is specifically unused by the spec. By default, all PyKMIP primitives start with this default tag; it's up to their instantiations or subclasses to specify their real tags, for whatever type of KMIP objects they actually represent. As a result, all of the encodings I take from the spec for these primitive tests are tweaked to make sure their tags line up properly with what my primitives will actual encode to. Hence the discrepancy.

We iterate over all the tag specs provided by the user. We only override the 'instance' tag spec. All other tag specs are appended, because `instance` is the only resource type Ray provides by default. With that said, the only thing we need to check is that the tags for the user-specified `instance` resource type are being overridden properly.

Below, we iterate over the user-specified tags if resource type is `instance`. We check if the tag exists. If it does, we only set the value provided by the user for that tag (e.g. `Name`). If it doesn't exist, we append the tag to the list of `instance` tags.
